sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The RIGHT action caused gameplay changes (32 cells changed). Let me check what moved by comparing the before and after frames. The green block likely moved since it seems to be the player. Let me inspect the current segmentation to see what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what moved
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation

print("BEFORE nodes (key ones):")
for node in before_seg['nodes']:
    if node['id'] not in [0, 11]:  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")

print("\nAFTER nodes (key ones):")
for node in after_seg['nodes']:
    if node['id'] not in [0, 11]:  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Find what moved by comparing boundaries
print("\nChanges detected:")
before_positions = {node['id']: node['boundary'] for node in before_seg['nodes']}
after_positions = {node['id']: node['boundary'] for node in after_seg['nodes']}

# Match by color and hash to track movement
before_by_hash = {}
for node in before_seg['nodes']:
    before_by_hash[node['hash']] = node['boundary']
    
after_by_hash = {}
for node in after_seg['nodes']:
    after_by_hash[node['hash']] = node['boundary']

for h in set(before_by_hash.keys()) | set(after_by_hash.keys()):
    b = before_by_hash.get(h)
    a = after_by_hash.get(h)
    if b != a:
        print(f"  Hash {h}: {b} -> {a}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The green block (N, hash 14e56d99ee75e53d) and white block (W, hash 6542e0ff68943d38) have changed. Looking at the boundaries:
Before:
- Node 9 (W): [[48, 32], [48, 35]] - 2x2 white block at row 48, cols 32-35
- Node 10 (N): [[49, 32], [49, 35], [51, 35], [51, 32]] - 3x4 green block at rows 49-51, cols 32-35
After:
- Node 9 (N): [[48, 36], [48, 38], [51, 38], [51, 36]] - 4x3 green block at rows 48-51, cols 36-38
- Node 10 (W): [[48, 39], [51, 39]] - 4x1 white strip at rows 48-51, col 39
So the green/white player block moved from cols 32-35 to cols 36-39 (shifted right by 4 columns). The shape also changed - it seems to have rotated or reshaped. The green part went from being 3x4 at the bottom to 4x3, and the white part went from 2x2 on top to a 4x1 strip on the right side.
This looks like the player is the green/white combined entity. It moved right by 4 columns.
Now I need to figure out the goal. Let me explore more to understand what the blue squares and the horizontal bar are for. Let me try moving up to see if the player can navigate toward the blue squares.
Wait, actually looking more carefully at the boundaries:
- Before: green at rows 49-51, cols 32-35; white at row 48, cols 32-35
- After: green at rows 48-51, cols 36-38; white at rows 48-51, col 39
The player moved right and also changed shape. This could be a character that rotates or changes orientation when moving in different directions.
Let me try moving UP to see if I can navigate toward the blue squares. The blue squares are at:
- Left: row 28-31, col 16-19
- Center: row 36-39, col 32-35
- Right: row 24-27, col 44-47
The horizontal bar is at row 28-31, col 28-39.
The player is now at rows 48-51, cols 36-39. Let me try moving UP to navigate toward the center blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The green/white player moved right by 4 columns and changed shape
# Let me try moving UP to navigate toward the blue squares
# Player is now at rows 48-51, cols 36-39
# Center blue square is at row 36-39, col 32-35

result = action(['UP'])
print("Result:", result)
print("Board changed:", result.get('board_changed'))
print("Gameplay changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed'))

# Check player position after UP
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
